title: Hyperemesis gravidarum
summary: A severe form of nausea and vomiting in pregnancy that usually begins in the first trimester and can lead to dehydration, weight loss, electrolyte problems, and inability to keep down food or fluids.
symptomPatterns: Vomiting many times per day or persistent retching
Inability to keep down fluids or food
Dizziness, weakness, low urine output, dark urine, or signs of dehydration
Weight loss or rapid worsening beyond typical morning sickness
typicalTiming: Often starts around 4 to 7 weeks
Usually improves by 12 to 20 weeks but may last longer
Needs urgent assessment when dehydration or reduced urine output develops
differentiators: Typical nausea and vomiting in pregnancy usually still allows some oral intake
Hyperemesis gravidarum is more severe, often with dehydration and functional decline
Consider molar pregnancy, multiple gestation, thyroid disease, or infection if course is atypical
suggestedWorkup: Electrolytes, renal function, and weight trend
Consider ultrasound if diagnosis is uncertain or symptoms are unusually severe
carePath: Same-day assessment if fluids cannot be kept down
Hospital treatment may be needed for IV fluids, antiemetics, and electrolyte replacement
Review medication safety in pregnancy with a clinician
pregnancyImpact: Severe dehydration and malnutrition can affect maternal health
Early treatment helps prevent complications and repeated hospital visits

citations.title: Hyperemesis gravidarum overview
citations.publisher: Guy's and St Thomas' NHS Foundation Trust

title: Preeclampsia, eclampsia, and HELLP spectrum
summary: Pregnancy-related hypertension after 20 weeks can progress to organ injury, seizures, stroke, liver complications, placental problems, and fetal compromise. Headache, vision change, right upper abdominal pain, shortness of breath, or major swelling make this especially urgent.
symptomPatterns: Blood pressure elevation after 20 weeks of pregnancy
Severe or persistent headache
Blurred vision, flashing lights, or other visual symptoms
Right upper abdominal or epigastric pain, nausea, or vomiting
Shortness of breath or sudden swelling of face and hands
typicalTiming: Usually after 20 weeks of pregnancy
Can also present postpartum
HELLP can present abruptly and sometimes without classic findings
differentiators: Gestational hypertension lacks the broader organ-injury pattern of preeclampsia
HELLP may include abdominal pain, nausea, bruising, and lab abnormalities even before obvious hypertension is recognized
AFLP can overlap with nausea, abdominal pain, jaundice, and liver dysfunction
carePath: Urgent or emergent obstetric evaluation is appropriate for suspected preeclampsia with symptoms
Seizure, severe hypertension, or breathing difficulty requires emergency care now
Do not rely on symptom monitoring alone when red flags are present
pregnancyImpact: Can reduce blood supply to the fetus
Increases risk for seizure, stroke, liver injury, placental abruption, and maternal-fetal morbidity

citations.title: What are the symptoms of preeclampsia, eclampsia, & HELLP syndrome?

title: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy
summary: A pregnancy-related liver condition where itching is often the dominant symptom, commonly affecting the palms and soles and often worse at night, usually without a primary rash. It needs obstetric review because it can affect the pregnancy.
symptomPatterns: Intense itching, especially on hands and feet
Little or no primary rash despite severe itch
Itching worse at night
Dark urine, pale stool, or jaundice in some cases
typicalTiming: Often starts from around 28 weeks
Can begin earlier
May worsen as pregnancy progresses
dangerSignals: Worsening generalized itch with no rash
Coexisting abdominal pain or reduced fetal movement
differentiators: Unlike pemphigoid gestationis or PUPPP, ICP usually does not start with obvious primary skin lesions
Palms and soles with nocturnal itching strongly raise concern
Normal-looking skin can still occur despite clinically important disease
suggestedWorkup: Obstetric review and fetal monitoring per clinician judgment
Medication review and repeat labs if symptoms persist
carePath: Prompt obstetric evaluation is appropriate for suspected ICP
Do not dismiss severe itching in pregnancy when there is little or no rash
Urgent care is appropriate if there are other concerning symptoms such as jaundice or reduced fetal movement
pregnancyImpact: Associated with pregnancy risk and requires monitoring
Early recognition helps guide medication and delivery planning

citations.title: Itching and intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy

title: Pemphigoid gestationis
summary: A rare autoimmune blistering disorder of pregnancy with intense itch, usually starting in the second or third trimester or shortly postpartum. It often begins around the umbilicus and can progress from urticarial plaques to vesicles and tense blisters.
symptomPatterns: Intense itch that may precede the rash
Papules or urticarial plaques around the belly button
Progression to vesicles or tense blisters
Spread across abdomen and extremities with face and mucosa often spared
typicalTiming: Usually second or third trimester
Can occur in any trimester or postpartum
May flare around delivery and recur earlier in later pregnancies
dangerSignals: Severe widespread itch with blister formation
Need for differentiation from other pregnancy dermatoses or drug eruptions
Any associated signs of infection, fever, or reduced fetal movement
differentiators: Periumbilical involvement is a classic clue and helps separate it from PUPPP, which often spares the umbilicus
Direct immunofluorescence is the key confirmatory test
Unlike ICP, there are primary skin lesions rather than isolated itch
suggestedWorkup: Consider BP180 antibody testing where available
Review for secondary infection and pregnancy monitoring needs
carePath: Prompt specialist assessment is appropriate when blistering or classic periumbilical lesions appear
Escalate urgently if rash is severe, rapidly progressive, or diagnosis is uncertain
Treatment commonly involves symptom control and corticosteroid-based plans directed by clinicians
pregnancyImpact: Some literature links pemphigoid gestationis with preterm birth and small-for-gestational-age infants
Maternal disease can recur in later pregnancies

title: PUPPP / polymorphic eruption of pregnancy
summary: A common benign itchy rash of late pregnancy, also called polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, typically appearing in stretch marks in the third trimester. It is very uncomfortable but usually not linked to poor maternal or fetal outcomes.
symptomPatterns: Intensely itchy papules and plaques
Often starts in abdominal stretch marks
Usually spares the umbilicus
Can spread to thighs, buttocks, and trunk
typicalTiming: Usually third trimester
Sometimes immediate postpartum
Often first pregnancy or multiple gestation
dangerSignals: Diagnostic uncertainty with blistering or umbilical involvement
Need to distinguish from pemphigoid gestationis or other dermatoses
Any systemic illness, fever, jaundice, or marked skin pain argues for a different process
differentiators: Usually spares the umbilicus, unlike pemphigoid gestationis
Primary lesions are papules and plaques rather than classic tense blisters
No primary rash is less consistent with PUPPP and should raise ICP or other alternatives
suggestedWorkup: Consider biopsy or immunofluorescence if diagnosis is unclear
Review for cholestasis or blistering disorders if features are atypical
carePath: Symptom-focused care is common once more serious mimics are excluded
Specialist review is useful if there are blisters, severe spread, or uncertainty
pregnancyImpact: Usually self-limited and not associated with fetal harm
Main impact is maternal discomfort and sleep disruption

citations.title: Pruritic Urticarial Papules and Plaques of Pregnancy
Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy: review of literature

title: Ectopic pregnancy
summary: A pregnancy located outside the uterus, usually in a fallopian tube. Early symptoms may include pelvic pain and vaginal bleeding, but rupture can cause internal bleeding and become a life-threatening emergency.
symptomPatterns: Pelvic pain or lower abdominal pain, sometimes one-sided
Light vaginal bleeding or spotting
Shoulder pain, rectal pressure, dizziness, or fainting if rupturing
Pregnancy symptoms may still be present
typicalTiming: Usually early pregnancy
Often before an intrauterine pregnancy has been confirmed by ultrasound
differentiators: One-sided pain plus bleeding in early pregnancy is a major warning pattern
Threatened miscarriage may also bleed, but ectopic must be ruled out urgently
Pregnancy test and ultrasound are required for diagnosis
suggestedWorkup: Immediate pregnancy test if status is unknown
Urgent obstetric or emergency assessment if symptoms are concerning
carePath: Emergency assessment is appropriate when rupture is possible
Do not wait at home if severe pain, fainting, or shoulder pain develops
pregnancyImpact: Cannot continue as a healthy pregnancy in the ectopic location
Delay in treatment can cause life-threatening bleeding

title: Threatened miscarriage or early pregnancy loss
summary: Vaginal bleeding with or without cramping in the first 20 weeks can represent threatened miscarriage or pregnancy loss, but not all bleeding means the pregnancy will be lost. Ectopic pregnancy and heavy-bleeding emergencies still have to be ruled out.
symptomPatterns: Vaginal bleeding or spotting in early pregnancy
Pelvic cramps or low back pain
Passing tissue or clots in some cases
Pregnancy symptoms may decrease, but this is not always reliable
typicalTiming: Before 20 weeks
Most losses occur early in pregnancy
differentiators: Threatened miscarriage can still continue as a viable pregnancy
Ectopic pregnancy must be excluded when bleeding and pain coexist
Ultrasound and serial hCG often guide the distinction
suggestedWorkup: Focused obstetric history and bleeding amount
Blood type review when clinically appropriate
carePath: Same-day evaluation is reasonable for new bleeding in pregnancy
Emergency care is appropriate for heavy bleeding, fainting, severe pain, or fever
pregnancyImpact: Pregnancy loss is common and emotionally significant
Timely evaluation helps identify whether urgent treatment is needed

title: Urinary tract infection and pyelonephritis in pregnancy
summary: UTIs are common in pregnancy and can progress from lower urinary symptoms to kidney infection. Pregnancy increases the stakes because untreated infection can contribute to preterm birth and severe maternal complications.
symptomPatterns: Burning with urination, frequency, urgency, or lower abdominal pressure
Cloudy, bloody, or foul-smelling urine
Back or flank pain, fever, shaking chills, nausea, or vomiting when kidney infection develops
typicalTiming: Can occur during any trimester and postpartum
Pyelonephritis often presents more abruptly with systemic illness
differentiators: Lower UTI symptoms center on dysuria, urgency, and bladder discomfort
Fever, flank pain, vomiting, or systemic illness suggest pyelonephritis
Pregnancy makes asymptomatic bacteriuria clinically important because progression can occur
suggestedWorkup: CBC and broader sepsis workup if ill appearing
carePath: Prompt clinician review is appropriate for urinary symptoms during pregnancy
Emergency or hospital-level care is appropriate if fever, flank pain, vomiting, or systemic illness appears
pregnancyImpact: Untreated infection can contribute to preterm birth and growth problems
Kidney infection can cause sepsis, blood clotting problems, and respiratory complications

citations.title: Urinary Tract Infections in Pregnant Individuals

title: Deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism in pregnancy
summary: Pregnancy and the postpartum period increase clot risk. One-sided leg swelling or pain raises concern for DVT, while sudden breathing difficulty, chest pain, or coughing blood raises concern for pulmonary embolism, which is an emergency.
symptomPatterns: Pain, swelling, tenderness, warmth, or redness in one leg
Sudden trouble breathing
Chest pain or tightness
Coughing blood or sudden collapse
typicalTiming: Can occur at any stage of pregnancy and up to 6 weeks postpartum
Risk is higher with thrombophilia, obesity, severe infection, surgery, or prior clot
differentiators: Symmetric mild leg swelling in pregnancy is common, but one-sided painful swelling is concerning
Chest symptoms should not be dismissed as anxiety or reflux without evaluation when clot is possible
carePath: Possible pulmonary embolism requires emergency care immediately
Possible DVT requires same-day urgent assessment
pregnancyImpact: Pulmonary embolism can be fatal
Rapid recognition changes treatment and outcomes

title: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy
summary: A rare but serious third-trimester liver emergency that can present with nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, malaise, jaundice, and rapidly progressive liver dysfunction. It can overlap clinically with preeclampsia or HELLP.
symptomPatterns: Third-trimester nausea and vomiting
Upper abdominal or right-sided abdominal pain
Malaise, headache, jaundice, confusion, or worsening illness
Possible overlap with hypertension or proteinuria
typicalTiming: Usually between 30 and 38 weeks
Often presents late and can worsen quickly
differentiators: May mimic HELLP or preeclampsia but liver failure features become prominent
Needs urgent lab evaluation and obstetric management
carePath: Emergency hospital assessment is appropriate
Do not treat progressive jaundice or confusion in late pregnancy as routine
pregnancyImpact: Can progress to acute liver failure and multi-organ dysfunction
Requires rapid maternal stabilization and obstetric planning
